title: Dyeing with agrimony
subtitle: A soft warm yellow from a common hedgerow plant on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Agrimony (Agrimonia eupatoria) is a common British hedgerow plant that yields a warm pale yellow to gold on alum-mordanted wool. The whole plant above ground is used, harvested when in flower. The result is a soft, natural yellow that complements other plant dye colours.
sourceNotes: Agrimony dyeing documented in Pellew, Dyes and Dyeing (1913, public domain, Internet Archive). The plant's flavonoid content gives a soft yellow on alum-mordanted wool; historical records in British dye manuals describe its use in cottage wool dyeing.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that forms a bond between the wool fibre and the dye. Alum is the standard mordant for most plant yellows and is low in hazard for home use.
The pot of water containing the dissolved or extracted dye material into which the mordanted fibre is placed. Temperature and time control the depth and consistency of the colour result.

content.text: Agrimony is one of the easier British hedgerow plants to collect and use as a dye. The whole above-ground plant is gathered in summer when the small yellow flowers are open. It requires a standard alum
and produces a soft, warm yellow on wool and silk. The result is lighter than weld or dyer's broom but combines well with other natural dye colours in a layered

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Use dedicated dye-pots. Wear gloves and apron. Alum is low-hazard; keep out of reach of food and children.

content.text: Dissolve 15 g alum in hot water and add to the dye-pot with enough cold water to cover the fibre loosely. Add the pre-wetted wool and raise the temperature to 85°C over 30 minutes. Hold for 45 minutes. Allow to cool before removing the wool.

Pack fresh agrimony loosely into a clean dye-pot. Cover with cold water. Bring slowly to 80°C and hold for 45 minutes, then strain out the plant material. If using dried agrimony, reduce the quantity to 150 g and soak the plant material in cold water for 2 hours before heating.

Add the mordanted, still-damp wool to the strained dye bath. Bring slowly to 75°C and hold for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally. Remove the wool, rinse in water of the same temperature, then progressively cooler water. Dry in shade.
The alum mordant bath and the agrimony dye bath can be composted or disposed of via the foul drain in small home quantities. Neither presents significant hazard at these volumes.

items.problem: The colour is very pale despite using the correct amount of plant material
items.cause: The agrimony was collected too early in the season, before flowering
items.fix: Collect agrimony when the raceme of flowers is fully open in mid to late summer. Early-season green plant material gives significantly less colour than flowering tops.
